text: The surgeon demonstrates the technique of pulling the suture to make the loop smaller, preparing to introduce the needle inside and take the suture out, ensuring the needle end is carefully extracted.
In da Vinci robotic surgery, the surgeon pulls the needle end of the suture to lock it, using either an endoskeletal or half-circle needle, before starting continuous suturing.

The surgeon uses wrist articulation to pull and tighten the suture without locking it, then takes another bite for continuous suturing on the serous surface of the myoma. This technique is also applied for gastric placation and peritoneal closure in hernia surgery.

The surgeon uses continuous suturing to close an approximately fifteen-centimeter intraperitoneal line, holding the needle at the junction of two-thirds and one-third of its length. The needle is aligned by passing it from one robotic hand to another, similar to open surgery techniques.

The surgeon demonstrates the termination of a suture knot using the Aberdeen technique, creating a loop within a loop without applying the 'C' shape. The process involves passing the suture from inside to form another loop.

During the everting termination of the suture, the surgeon pulls the non-needle end to lock the loop within loop, ensuring it is tight. The needle end is then taken, with careful handling of the suture to prevent trapping.
